summary.brief: Kos v. Lawrence + Memorial Hospital. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: The plaintiffs, K and her husband, sought to recover damages from the defendants, G, a physician, and G’s medical practice, for personal injuries that K had suffered in connection with G’s alleged negligence in, inter alia, failing to perform a proper and adequate episiotomy repair after the birth of the plaintiffs’ son.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: G had performed an episiotomy to facilitate the delivery of the plaintiffs’ son. After the delivery, G evaluated K and diagnosed her with a third degree episiotomy extension, which G repaired.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: At trial, the plaintiffs’ expert witness, Y, testified that the standard of care requires that a physician, after performing an episiotomy, correctly diagnose and repair the episiotomy and any extension thereof, which must involve a thorough rectal examination before the repair.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiffs' expert witness

evidence_index.text: According to the defendants’ expert, L, G complied with the standard of care, which required that the rectal exam be performed after rather than before the episiotomy repair.
evidence_index.speaker: Defendants' expert witness

evidence_index.text: The jury returned a verdict in favor of the defendants, finding that the plaintiffs had sustained their burden of establishing the standard of care but failed to sustain their burden of establishing that G breached the standard of care.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Although the trial court improperly instructed the jury on the acceptable alternatives doctrine, that charge was harmless under the circumstances of the present case, and this court declined the plaintiffs’ request to abolish that doctrine.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient was in labor and had been pushing for approximately two hours. Physician decided to use a vacuum to assist in delivery, which was unsuccessful, leading to the performance of a median episiotomy.

focused_exam.perineum: Episiotomy performed, inspection for extension required

requestables.name: Post-delivery examination
requestables.description: Examination of the episiotomy site and rectal mucosa

reveal.result_summary: No breaks or defects in rectal mucosa initially noted
reveal.clinical_significance: Initial examination did not reveal a fourth degree extension
reveal.decision_impact: Influenced decision to diagnose as third degree extension

requestables.name: Expert Testimony - Ling
requestables.description: Testimony regarding standard of care for episiotomy examination

reveal.result_summary: Postrepair examination preferred to avoid contamination
reveal.clinical_significance: Prone to infection if prerepair exam is done

key_events.event: Digital rectal examination performed after repair
Patient reports complications including pain and fistula

decision_points.prompt: When should the digital rectal examination be performed?

options.description: Perform digital rectal examination before episiotomy repair
options.clinical_reasoning: Ensures accurate diagnosis of the degree of extension

options.description: Perform digital rectal examination after episiotomy repair
options.clinical_reasoning: Reduces risk of contamination but may miss fourth degree extension

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant performed digital rectal examination after repair
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: To prevent contamination of the open wound

expected_action_court.description: Digital rectal examination should have been performed before repair
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established pre-repair examination as standard

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to perform pre-repair examination led to misdiagnosis
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Pre-repair examination would have identified fourth degree extension
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

key_findings: Jury found no breach of standard of care

factual_timeline.event: Patient gave birth at Lawrence + Memorial Hospital

tests_performed.result: No breaks or defects in rectal mucosa initially noted

allegations.allegation: Failure to identify a fourth degree extension
Failure to perform prerepair digital rectal exam

court_findings.finding: No breach of standard of care
No breach of standard of care
court_findings.legal_significance: Jury found no fourth degree extension
